title: CHP's Dual Rallies, Barrack's Triple Mandate, and Fidan's Regional Balancing Act
subhead: Ousted CHP leader Özel and reinstated Kılıçdaroğlu held rival Ankara gatherings, Trump gave Ambassador Barrack a Syria-Iraq portfolio on top of his Turkey post, and Foreign Minister Fidan told Nikkei Asia a US-Iran deal is 'closer than ever' — while Hormuz remains the real red line for Ankara.

sections.heading: CHP Crisis Deepens: Rival Rallies in Ankara
sections.body:
On Saturday, Turkey's main opposition Republican People's Party (CHP) split into two public camps in Ankara. Özgür Özel, removed from the party leadership by an Ankara court ruling on Thursday that annulled the 2023 party congress, addressed thousands of supporters near Güvenpark — just days after riot police used tear gas to evict him from CHP headquarters. The crowd repeatedly slammed his predecessor as a 'traitor,' according to Reuters and Yahoo News.

Meanwhile, Kemal Kılıçdaroğlu — provisionally reinstated as interim CHP leader by the same court — held a competing rally outside party headquarters to mark Eid al-Adha, releasing white doves after his address. He called for a new party leadership vote 'as soon as possible' and said 'whoever emerges victorious will be the legitimate leader,' according to Yahoo News.

Thursday's court ruling annulled the 2023 CHP congress that elected Özel, citing alleged irregularities. The CHP called it a 'judicial coup.' Human Rights Watch said Turkey's justice system has been 'weaponised' against the opposition, Reuters and Al-Monitor reported. The ruling also triggered a 6% stock market plunge and the lira hitting record lows before a partial Friday recovery, the Guardian editorial board noted.

Özel told supporters the dispute is not an internal party matter: 'This concerns Recep Tayyip Erdoğan and the people.' The broader crackdown has seen the jailing of Istanbul Mayor Ekrem İmamoğlu — arrested in March 2025 on corruption, espionage, and terrorism charges he denies — and hundreds of CHP officials detained since 2024, according to Al-Monitor.
sections.whyItMatters: The court-ordered removal of a popular opposition leader and the physical storming of party headquarters marks a new escalation in the systematic constraining of Turkey's opposition. The split between Özel's street legitimacy and Kılıçdaroğlu's court-granted institutional authority risks fragmenting the only party that defeated Erdoğan's AKP in 2024 local elections.

sections.heading: Trump Gives Ambassador Barrack a Syria-Iraq Mandate Alongside Turkey
sections.body:
President Donald Trump announced on Sunday that US Ambassador to Turkey Tom Barrack will also serve as Special Presidential Envoy to Syria and Special Presidential Envoy to Iraq, while retaining his ambassadorship in Ankara. The announcement came via Truth Social amid ongoing US-Iran negotiations, Fox News reported.

Trump said Barrack 'has done an outstanding job' and will 'operate with the full backing of the United States Department of State.' Al Arabiya and MSN reported that the move signals growing US strategic cooperation with the governments of Syria and Iraq. The appointment consolidates three critical regional portfolios — Turkey, Syria, and Iraq — in a single diplomat, an unusual arrangement that places Ankara at the centre of Washington's Middle East diplomatic architecture.

Secretary of State Marco Rubio had previously signalled Barrack would take on a Damascus-focused role, according to MSN. The expanded Iraq mandate suggests the administration sees Turkey as the operational hub for managing both post-Assad Syria and an Iraq still navigating Iranian and American pressures simultaneously.
sections.whyItMatters: Consolidating the Syria, Iraq, and Turkey portfolios under one ambassador signals Washington's intent to treat these as a single strategic theatre — with Ankara as the base of operations. For Turkey, it elevates Barrack's already significant role and embeds US Syria-Iraq diplomacy inside the embassy, giving Ankara proximity and potential leverage over both files.

sections.heading: Fidan: US-Iran Deal 'Closer Than Ever,' But Hormuz Is the Priority
sections.body:
Foreign Minister Hakan Fidan told Nikkei Asia that a US-Iran agreement is 'closer than at any other time,' with both sides wanting a positive conclusion. But he stressed that for Ankara, the overriding concern is the Strait of Hormuz, where Iran's de facto blockade is 'putting too much pressure on both' the US and Iran, TRT World and Ilkha News reported.

Fidan warned that the Hormuz standoff's impact on 'energy security, food security, and rising prices' makes it a higher priority than the nuclear file. Turkey, as a major energy importer, faces direct exposure to any disruption in Gulf shipping lanes.

On Israel, Fidan said Turkey's trade suspension was conditional on Israel ending the killing of Palestinians and allowing humanitarian access to Gaza, adding: 'We want to achieve a two-state solution.' He characterised Israeli politicians' portrayal of Turkey as a strategic threat as domestic politicking, stating: 'Everybody knows Israel is not after its security, but after more land.'

Fidan also outlined a regional vision involving cooperation among Turkey, Saudi Arabia, Egypt, Pakistan, and Gulf states — with Iran potentially included 'when things come back to normal' — and Israel joining only if it accepts a Palestinian state on 1967 borders, according to TRT World.
sections.whyItMatters: Ankara is positioning itself as both mediator and stakeholder: publicly nudging the US-Iran process while privately prioritising the Hormuz shipping lane that directly determines Turkey's energy costs. Fidan's framing also makes clear that Turkey's trade freeze on Israel is conditional, not permanent — keeping the diplomatic door ajar.

sections.heading: Euphrates Flooding Displaces Thousands in Eastern Syria
sections.body:
Rising Euphrates River levels have flooded thousands of hectares of farmland and knocked out at least 60 water stations in Deir ez-Zor province, eastern Syria, The New Arab and Asharq Al-Awsat reported. Syrian President Ahmed al-Sharaa visited the province on Friday to assess the damage.

Syria's energy minister, Mohammad al-Bashir, said Turkey issued late warnings about rising water levels and described the current releases as 'unprecedented in the past three decades.' Technical teams at the Euphrates Dam have reduced discharge by 100 cubic metres per second through partial closure of one spillway gate, with further reductions expected, The New Arab reported.

Deir ez-Zor governor Ziad al-Ayesh activated an emergency response committee and round-the-clock operations room. Precautionary evacuations have been carried out in high-risk areas, temporary shelters established, and medical teams dispatched to the eastern bank, where health infrastructure is more limited. The minister for emergency and disaster management, Raed al-Saleh, said damage in Deir ez-Zor exceeded that in neighbouring Raqqa province.
sections.whyItMatters: Turkey controls the upstream headwaters of the Euphrates through its dam network. The accusation of late warnings — and the characterization of current releases as a 30-year high — feeds an existing narrative across Syria and Iraq that Ankara uses water as leverage. With Barrack now holding the Syria envoy portfolio, this file gains a direct US diplomatic channel.

sections.heading: Öcalan Calls for Legal Framework as Kurdish Peace Process Stalls
sections.body:
Jailed PKK founder Abdullah Öcalan issued a call from İmralı prison island urging the Turkish state to establish a legal framework for the stalled peace process, warning that delays 'only generate risk,' Kurdistan24 and AFP reported. Speaking via a DEM Party delegation that visited on May 24, Öcalan said there is 'no time to lose.'

The statement comes after the PKK formally renounced its four-decade armed struggle in 2025 and, on May 5, 2026, announced a name change to the 'Apoist Movement' — pending reciprocal legal steps from Ankara that have not materialised, Kurdistan24 reported.

Öcalan specifically referenced the CHP headquarters raid, asking: 'Is breaking into the headquarters of a political party by smashing the door with a sledgehammer something that should happen in a democracy?' He linked the Kurdish peace process to broader democratic reform, calling the development of Turkey's 'democratic character' the 'most urgent priority facing the country.'
sections.whyItMatters: Öcalan is tying the Kurdish peace process to the broader democratic crisis — explicitly connecting his call for a legal framework to the opposition crackdown. With Erdoğan potentially needing pro-Kurdish DEM Party votes for constitutional amendments allowing a new presidential term, the peace process remains a live bargaining chip — but the crackdown on CHP makes DEM Party cooperation more costly domestically.

sections.heading: COP31 Preparations: Cyprus Exclusion Dispute
sections.body:
European officials criticised Turkey after reports that Cyprus was excluded from several COP31 climate summit preparation meetings, Greek City Times reported. The dispute resurfaces a long-standing pattern: Turkey does not recognise the Republic of Cyprus and routinely blocks its participation in international forums hosted on Turkish soil.

COP31, to be held in Turkey, was seen as an opportunity for Ankara to showcase its climate credentials and regional leadership. The Cyprus exclusion, however, threatens to draw EU criticism and complicate Turkey's relationship with Brussels at a moment when it is seeking strategic relevance on multiple fronts — from Hormuz mediation to Syria diplomacy.
sections.whyItMatters: The COP31-Cyprus row is a reminder that Turkey's EU accession norms and its de facto non-recognition of Cyprus remain fundamentally incompatible. At a time when Ankara is courting international legitimacy as a regional mediator, excluding an EU member state from a multilateral climate summit on its soil undercuts that positioning.

sections.heading: Fidan Touts Turkey-Japan Drone Cooperation and Critical Minerals
sections.body:
Speaking to Nikkei Asia, Foreign Minister Fidan said Turkey and Japan have 'complementary capabilities' in unmanned aerial systems and anti-drone technologies, and proposed joint development and co-production of drones, TRT World reported. Turkey has emerged as a major drone exporter in recent years, with Baykar platforms combat-proven in multiple theatres.

Beyond defence, Fidan pointed to untapped cooperation in energy, digital transformation, aerospace, robotics, and supply chain resilience. On critical minerals, he said Ankara's objective extends beyond extraction to 'producing high-value-added intermediate and end products,' suggesting Turkey wants Japanese investment not just in mining but in downstream processing, according to TRT World.

Fidan also said progress had been made on a social security agreement between the two countries, expressing hope it could be finalised soon. The broader message: Turkey is using the Nikkei Asia interview to signal to Tokyo and Washington's Asian allies that it is a serious defence-industrial partner, not just a regional actor.
sections.whyItMatters: Turkey is actively diversifying its defence-industrial partnerships beyond NATO's traditional axis. A Japan drone co-production deal would signal that Turkey's UAV capabilities have reached a level where even a US treaty ally sees value in collaboration — and would complicate any future US efforts to constrain Turkey's drone exports.

bottomLine: A day that crystallised Turkey's contradictory positions: an opposition leader ousted by court order while his predecessor releases doves outside party headquarters, a US ambassador handed three countries' portfolios from his Ankara desk, and a foreign minister simultaneously claiming the US-Iran deal is imminent and that Hormuz shipping — not nukes — is what really matters to Turkey. Öcalan's intervention linked the Kurdish peace process directly to the democratic crisis, raising the stakes on both tracks. Meanwhile, Euphrates flooding in Syria puts Turkey's upstream dam management back under regional scrutiny just as Barrack takes on the Syria envoy role. The pieces are moving fast; none of them are moving toward democratic normalisation.
sources: Reuters, Yahoo News, Al-Monitor, The Guardian, Fox News, Al Arabiya, TRT World, Ilkha News, The New Arab, Asharq Al-Awsat, Greek City Times, Kurdistan24, MSN
